source.title: Saliency, switching, attention and control: a network model of insula function

confidence_rationale: Canonical formulation of the salience network: AIC + dACC as coupled hub that detects salient events and switches between DMN and task-positive networks. This is the paper that provides the network-level glue tying the cingulate subdivisions together. High mapping fidelity, cited across thousands of later papers as the standard framework.
failure_signatures: A substrate that implements each cingulate subdivision as an isolated module without cross-coupling will fail the core salience-network prediction: that salient events should produce coordinated activity across AIC and dACC simultaneously, and that this coordination drives switching *between* other networks (DMN vs task-positive). ree-v3 has no network-switching primitive.

Menon & Uddin's four-mechanism decomposition (bottom-up salience detection, inter-network switching, anterior-posterior insula autonomic modulation, AIC-ACC coupling for rapid motor access) predicts that failure of ANY one mechanism compromises salience-driven behaviour. A minimal cingulate substrate that implements only one or two will have characteristic failure modes mapped to the missing mechanisms.

mapping.source_claim_statement: AIC is the integral hub of a 'salience network' that includes dACC (and their subcortical connections). The network's core function is to (1) detect salient events bottom-up from multiple modalities, (2) switch between other large-scale networks (DMN vs task-positive) to bring appropriate resources online, (3) modulate autonomic reactivity via anterior-posterior insula coupling, and (4) use strong AIC-ACC functional coupling to get rapid access to motor output. The salience network is functionally distinct from both the DMN and the central-executive network; it is the coordinator that selects between them.
mapping.ree_translation: This is the paper that provides the network-level specification for the new cingulate substrate. The architectural claim: the cingulate substrate is not a set of independent modules (ACC, MCC, AIC, PCC each doing their own thing) -- it is a *coordinated network* whose primary function is selecting between other large-scale processing modes. For ree-v3 this means (i) the cingulate cluster needs internal coupling (AIC-analog <-> dACC-analog <-> MCC-analog are co-active on salient events), (ii) its primary output is not local action-value modification but *selection between operating modes* (task execution vs replay vs consolidation), and (iii) the interrupt/switch function lives at the network level, not in any single module. This unifies several ree-v3 gaps: commit-state transitions, offline-phase entry, mode-dependent write gating, and urgency-interrupt all want to route through this network-switching substrate.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The salience-network formulation is popular but has been critiqued -- some argue it over-emphasises AIC and underspecifies the subcortical role, others that the 'switching' description is too crisp and real network transitions are more graded. The four-mechanism decomposition is Menon & Uddin's own synthesis, not independently validated. ree-v3 should adopt the basic structure (salience network switches between operating modes) but treat the specific four mechanisms as architectural hypotheses to be tested, not prescriptive.
mapping.source_context: Network-model review synthesising human fMRI, resting-state connectivity, clinical data, and some primate work. One of the foundational papers in the current 'triple-network' framework (DMN, CEN, salience).

confidence_components.notes: Field-defining paper (cited >10,000 times). The triple-network framework is now standard and provides clean computational abstractions ree-v3 can adopt. Transfer risk is low because the network-level framing translates naturally to operating-mode selection in an embodied agent.
